description:
A parent task whose children are not all closed should not surface in `bl ready` (or `bl prime`'s ready section). It cannot be picked up — `bl claim` on an epic is wrong work; the children are what's actually claimable. Today `ready_queue` in src/ready.rs:6 filters only on status / claim / `depends_on`, ignoring parenthood, so epics get surfaced and a shell wrapper that maps "ready" → "spawn an agent" wastes a session.

## Behavior

- A task with any non-closed child is hidden from `bl ready` and `bl prime`'s ready section.
- It re-appears in `ready` the moment the last child closes — at that point the parent itself is the only remaining work and `bl close <epic>` is the next action.
- `bl claim <id>` against a parent with live children should fail fast with a clear error pointing at the live children (so direct claims by id also don't silently misroute).
- Independent of task `type` ("epic", "task", etc.) — the rule is "has live children", structural not tag-based.

## Out of scope

- Changing how a parent auto-closes when its last child closes (current behavior preserved — closing the last child does not close the parent; the parent must be explicitly closed).
- Changing what `bl list` / `bl dep tree` show. Those are inventory views; ready is the "spawn me an agent" view.

## Why

Hit while picking up bl-f021 (epic) via a shell wrapper that reads `bl ready` and starts a session per id. The epic surfaced as ready alongside its real children; the wrapper had no way to know the difference. `ready` should mean "claimable right now," not "open with deps satisfied."

## Tests gated

- `ready_queue` excludes a parent with at least one non-closed child.
- `ready_queue` includes a parent once all its children are closed.
- `ready_queue` is unchanged for leaf tasks (no children).
- `bl claim` against a parent with live children errors with a message naming a live child id.
- `bl prime --json` ready array matches `bl ready --json` (same filter applies in both surfaces).
title: bl ready hides parents with live children
